title: HTML Forms
description: Learn how to build a real HTML form: inputs, labels, required fields, radio buttons, and checkboxes.
congratsText: Great work! You can now build a real HTML form.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Build a form with properly linked labels and input fields
  • Use different input types and built-in checks like required and placeholder
  • Group choices correctly with radio buttons and checkboxes

sections.title: What Is a Form?

quiz.question: What is the main job of the <form> tag?
options: It groups input fields so they can be sent together
It changes the page's background color
It loads a new page automatically every second
It stores images for faster page loading

sections.lesson:
What Is a Form?
A form lets users type information and send it somewhere — like a search box, a login screen, or a signup page. The <form> tag wraps all the input fields together.
Inside a form, the <input> tag creates a field a user can fill in. <input> is a self-closing tag — it has no separate closing tag like </input>.
<form> Name: <input type="text"> </form>
Forms are how a page collects data from a real person, instead of just showing static text.

sections.title: Labels and Accessibility

quiz.question: Why will clicking this label NOT move the cursor into the input?
options: The label's for value does not match the input's id
Labels never connect to input fields at all
The input is missing its type attribute
The label text is spelled wrong

sections.lesson:
Labels and Accessibility
A <label> tag connects text to an input field. Clicking the label text moves the cursor into the matching field.
<label for="email">Email:</label> <input type="text" id="email">
The connection works through matching values: the label's for must exactly match the input's id.
Without a matching label, users of screen readers may not know what a field is for.

sections.title: Common Input Types

quiz.question: What will the browser do with this unknown input type?
options: Treat it as a plain text field
Refuse to load the whole page
Delete the input tag automatically
Show a red error box on screen

sections.lesson:
Common Input Types
The type attribute changes both the look and the behavior of an input, not just its label.
  • type="email" checks for a valid email pattern
  • type="password" hides the typed characters
  • type="number" only accepts numbers
<input type="email"> <input type="password"> <input type="number">
If the browser does not know a type, it quietly treats the field as plain text.

sections.title: The required Attribute

quiz.question: When does the required attribute stop a form from submitting?
options: When the field is empty and the user clicks submit
Only after the page has fully reloaded
Only if the server sends back an error
It never actually stops a form from submitting

sections.lesson:
The required Attribute
Adding required to an input tells the browser: don't let the form submit until this field has a value.
<input type="text" required>
This check happens in the browser, before any data is sent anywhere. No extra code is needed to turn it on.
required is a good first check, but a real system should also check data again on the server, since browser checks can be skipped.

sections.title: Placeholder Text

quiz.question: If a user never types in a field but it shows placeholder text, is the field empty or filled?
options: Empty — placeholder text is only a hint
Filled — placeholder text counts as the value
Filled, but only for email-type fields
It depends on the browser being used

sections.lesson:
Placeholder Text
placeholder shows light gray hint text inside an empty field. It disappears the moment the user starts typing.
<input type="text" placeholder="e.g. Juan Dela Cruz">
Placeholder is not a value. If the user never types anything, the field is still considered empty when the form is sent.

sections.title: Radio Buttons

quiz.question: Can a user pick both of these radio buttons at the same time?
quiz.code:
<input type="radio" name="size" value="S"> Small
<input type="radio" name="color" value="M"> Medium
options: Yes, because they have different name values
No, radio buttons never allow two picks
No, because their value attributes differ
Yes, but only in older browsers

sections.lesson:
Radio Buttons
Radio buttons let a user pick only one option from a group. Every button in the same group must share the same name.
<input type="radio" name="size" value="S"> Small <input type="radio" name="size" value="M"> Medium
If the buttons have different names, the browser treats them as separate groups — and more than one can be picked at once.

sections.title: Checkboxes

quiz.question: What is the main difference between a checkbox and a radio button?
options: A checkbox allows more than one choice at once
A checkbox always requires a matching label
A checkbox cannot use the name attribute
A checkbox only works inside a table

sections.lesson:
Checkboxes
Checkboxes let a user pick any number of options — none, one, or several at once. This is the main difference from radio buttons.
<input type="checkbox" name="topping" value="cheese"> Cheese <input type="checkbox" name="topping" value="bacon"> Bacon
Checkboxes can share the same name too — the server just receives a list of every one the user checked.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: A signup form needs a required email field with a hint, plus a way to pick exactly one account type. Which combination is correct?
options: type="email" with required and placeholder, plus radio buttons sharing one name
type="text" with no attributes, plus checkboxes sharing one name
type="email" with no required, plus radio buttons with different names
type="password" with placeholder only, plus a single checkbox

sections.lesson:
Recap
A form collects data through <input> fields, each linked to a <label> by matching for and id.
  • type sets the kind of field (text, email, password, number)
  • required blocks submission until the field has a value
  • placeholder is a hint, not a real value
  • radio buttons share one name and allow only one pick; checkboxes allow several
<label for="email">Email:</label> <input type="email" id="email" required placeholder="you@example.com">
